caveats: a: The occupation source covers all NAICS 311800 establishments and excludes self-employed workers.
a: Commercial production bakers already use high-volume equipment that may be automated, so gross task exposure is not necessarily not-yet-automated opportunity.
a: The mapping is judgmental and wage-weighted from published occupation employment and wage data, not a directly observed AI task study.
rho: No 311812-specific five-year AI implementation cohort was located.
rho: FSMA scope and modified requirements vary by facility, but covered facilities must maintain documented controls and oversight.
e: The public operator is much larger than the target band and is evidence about business model, not the eligible share of LMM firms.
e: The provided firm count is margin-bridged using a subsector benchmark and may misclassify owner compensation or unusually capital-intensive plants.

s5: The cited public filing describes generational change as a competitive factor but does not quantify transactions.
q: Flowers Foods is a large branded operator and may have more brand leverage but also more concentrated national accounts than LMM firms.
q: No observed benefit-sharing dataset was located.
d5: The output projection is at four digits and cannot isolate commercial bakeries.
d5: Constant-quality demand can diverge from real output if product mix, waste, distribution services, or private-label share changes.
o: The measure concerns an accountable operator, not persistence of any particular independent supplier.
o: A customer bringing manufacturing in-house would reduce outsourced operator demand even though physical baking continues.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring wholesale product supply, generally priced by item, case, shipment, or negotiated customer program and delivered through direct-store-delivery or warehouse channels; some firms also manufacture under customer brands or foodservice specifications.
lens.excluded_activities: Retail bakeries, frozen non-bread pastry manufacturers, cookie and cracker manufacturers, immediate-consumption snack bars, captive internal bakery units without external customers, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market commercial bakeries that manufacture fresh or frozen bread, bread-type rolls, and other fresh bakery products and repeatedly supply external retail, foodservice, institutional, wholesale, vending, or contract-manufacturing customers.

a.bridge: BLS OEWS measures the broader four-digit NAICS 311800 population rather than commercial bakeries alone. I treated planning, ordering, inventory, customer service, administration, sales analysis, quality documentation, and management as substantially AI-exposed; production, packing, maintenance, sanitation, and physical distribution as much less exposed; then judgmentally wage-weighted the mix. Commercial bakeries may have more factory production and existing automation than the four-digit average, biasing exposure downward relative to the proxy, while whole-occupation mapping can overstate automatable task shares.
caveats: The occupation source covers all NAICS 311800 establishments and excludes self-employed workers.
Commercial production bakers already use high-volume equipment that may be automated, so gross task exposure is not necessarily not-yet-automated opportunity.
The mapping is judgmental and wage-weighted from published occupation employment and wage data, not a directly observed AI task study.
a.change_evidence: A 311812-specific payroll and task-time extract, split by plant size and existing automation, with workflow-level measurements for forecasting, scheduling, procurement, quality documentation, customer service, pricing, and logistics would materially move the range.
a.rationale: Production occupations are 49.70% of broader-industry employment, including bakers at 21.90%, food batchmakers at 7.97%, and packaging-machine operators at 6.71%; transportation and material moving is another 13.23%. Exposed work is concentrated in management, business, sales, office support, customer coordination, demand planning, maintenance planning, and quality records rather than physical baking and movement.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for broader NAICS 311800 from 62.1 billion chained 2017 dollars in 2024 to 74.4 billion in 2034. Taking the square root of the ten-year ratio yields a five-year ratio of about 1.094, used as the base for 311812. Because commercial bakeries are a major but not separately projected component and the four-digit series also includes retail bakeries, frozen pastries, cookies, crackers, and tortillas, segment mix could bias the proxy in either direction.
caveats: The output projection is at four digits and cannot isolate commercial bakeries.
Constant-quality demand can diverge from real output if product mix, waste, distribution services, or private-label share changes.
d5.change_evidence: Six-digit 311812 real shipment indexes and physical-volume data by fresh bread, rolls, pastries, foodservice, private label, and branded retail would materially narrow the projection.
d5.rationale: The official four-digit projection implies 1.8% compound annual real-output growth, while BLS projects baker employment growth of 6% over the decade and points to population, income, convenience, and specialty-product demand. Mature fresh packaged bread and channel competition justify a range that includes near-flat demand.

caveats: The public operator is much larger than the target band and is evidence about business model, not the eligible share of LMM firms.
The provided firm count is margin-bridged using a subsector benchmark and may misclassify owner compensation or unusually capital-intensive plants.
e.change_evidence: A representative 311812 firm sample with customer invoices, revenue recurrence, customer tenure, affiliate sales, and contract terms would directly measure eligibility.
e.rationale: Commercial bakeries manufacture for downstream business customers, and fresh-product replenishment naturally produces repeat orders. Public operator evidence shows recurring supermarket, convenience, restaurant, institutional, foodservice, wholesale, vending, and contract-manufacturing channels. A minority may depend on sporadic co-packing, internal affiliates, or customer/product concentration inconsistent with a transferable recurring-service lens.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 311812 ($8,524,989,489) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 311812 ($34,870,939,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1525 (margins.json[31]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Food Processing EBITDA/Sales 15.25% (dominant subsector of NAICS 31)); revenue/employee $308,486 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 311812); class <5: 853 firms, avg revenue/firm $338,216 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $51,578 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 440 firms, avg revenue/firm $736,405 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $112,302 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 424 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,681,184 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $256,381 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 505 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,259,321 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $802,046 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 143 firms, avg revenue/firm $44,871,441 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,842,895 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 82 firms, avg revenue/firm $298,451,561 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $45,513,863 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 143

caveats: The measure concerns an accountable operator, not persistence of any particular independent supplier.
A customer bringing manufacturing in-house would reduce outsourced operator demand even though physical baking continues.
o.change_evidence: Customer procurement studies measuring substitution from commercial-bakery supply to in-store production, bake-off systems, imports, or customer-owned plants, plus facility-level automation and outsourcing trends, would test operator displacement.
o.rationale: Bread and fresh bakery products require physical manufacturing, packaging, safety controls, replenishment, and delivery. Software can coordinate or optimize these functions but cannot supply edible products by itself, and covered facilities retain accountable food-safety obligations. Channel consolidation may move output between operators but does not eliminate an operator.

caveats: Flowers Foods is a large branded operator and may have more brand leverage but also more concentrated national accounts than LMM firms.
No observed benefit-sharing dataset was located.
q.change_evidence: Customer-contract reviews and five years of SKU-level price, promotion, rebate, volume, gross-margin, and cost data around automation initiatives, segmented by branded, private-label, foodservice, and co-manufacturing channels, would reveal realized retention.
q.rationale: Per-case and negotiated product pricing means savings are not mechanically passed through. However, large retailers and foodservice buyers have bargaining power, bakery competition turns on price and promotions as well as quality and availability, and contracts or renewal negotiations can transfer benefits to customers. Operators with differentiated brands or specialized products should retain more than commodity private-label suppliers.

caveats: No 311812-specific five-year AI implementation cohort was located.
FSMA scope and modified requirements vary by facility, but covered facilities must maintain documented controls and oversight.
rho.change_evidence: A multi-plant cohort reporting sustained labor-hour savings, forecast accuracy, waste, order fill, quality deviations, and audit findings twelve to twenty-four months after AI workflow deployment would directly test implementation.
rho.rationale: Data-rich, repetitive planning and administrative workflows should support meaningful implementation, especially in forecasting, production scheduling, purchasing, route and inventory exceptions, customer communication, and document review. Integration with ERP, plant controls, retailer systems, and quality records plus human accountability for food safety constrain autonomous deployment.

caveats: No source directly measures five-year control-transfer probability for eligible 311812 firms.
The cited public filing describes generational change as a competitive factor but does not quantify transactions.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of Axial, GF Data, PitchBook, CapIQ, BizBuySell, SBA 7(a), and specialist food-industry broker closes tagged to 311812, screened for continuing-operation control transfers and matched to the eligible firm population, would materially refine the estimate.
s5.rationale: Independent regional and family-owned bakeries face succession and strategic-sale pressure, but the qualifying probability must exclude plant closures, distressed liquidations, internal reorganizations, minority financings, and family transfers without control change. Capital intensity and customer concentration can narrow the buyer pool.

principal_driver: Recurring replenishment relationships create durable external-customer demand and data-rich planning workflows.
principal_weakness: Physical production dominates labor, while powerful downstream buyers can recapture savings through price and commercial terms.
